title: Ambu King Vision aBlade Video Laryngoscope - Display Does Not Power On
description: Troubleshooting a King Vision aBlade display that will not power on due to battery, contact, accessory, or external damage issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the device is not needed for an active airway procedure before troubleshooting. If the King Vision display fails during setup or patient care, provide clinical staff with another ready video laryngoscope or direct laryngoscope per facility procedure. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed by a nonfunctional display.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Power Failure
steps.instructions: Press the power button and observe the display. Check whether the unit is completely blank, briefly flashes, shows a low battery indication, or powers off immediately. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed and documented.

steps.title: Check Battery Installation
steps.instructions: Open the battery compartment and verify the batteries are installed in the correct polarity. Remove and reinstall the batteries, ensuring they seat firmly against the contacts. Expected outcome: Display powers on normally if the issue was caused by loose or incorrectly installed batteries. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Replace with Known-Good Batteries
steps.instructions: Install fresh, facility-approved batteries. Do not rely on batteries removed from another unknown device. Expected outcome: Display powers on if the original batteries were depleted, weak, or expired. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Check the battery contacts for corrosion, bent terminals, fluid residue, or visible contamination. Do not scrape aggressively or use excessive force. Clean only according to facility-approved procedure. Expected outcome: Contacts appear clean, intact, and able to maintain battery pressure.

steps.title: Inspect the Battery Door and Housing
steps.instructions: Verify the battery door closes securely and is not cracked, loose, or missing. Inspect the display housing for impact damage, liquid intrusion, damaged seams, or evidence of being dropped. Expected outcome: Display housing and battery compartment are physically intact.

steps.title: Remove the Blade and Retest the Display
steps.instructions: Disconnect the aBlade and attempt to power on the display by itself. A damaged or improperly seated blade should not be allowed to confuse the initial power check. Expected outcome: Display powers on without the blade if the attached blade was contributing to the issue. If resolved, replace the blade and retest.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good aBlade
steps.instructions: Attach a known-good compatible aBlade and power the unit on again. Confirm the blade locks in properly and does not place stress on the display connection. Expected outcome: Display powers on and produces an image if the issue was related to the blade or blade connection. If resolved, remove the defective blade from use.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Power
steps.instructions: With fresh batteries installed, gently move the display in normal handling positions. Do not twist, flex, or stress the housing. Watch for flickering, rebooting, or power loss. Expected outcome: Display remains powered without interruption. Intermittent behavior suggests battery-contact, power-button, or internal connection failure.

steps.title: Confirm Cleaning or Fluid Exposure History
steps.instructions: Ask whether the device was recently cleaned, soaked, sprayed heavily, dropped, or stored with batteries installed. Excess liquid or residue near the battery compartment can prevent reliable power-on. Expected outcome: Any likely external exposure is identified and documented.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Internal Repair During Floor Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: If the display remains blank after fresh batteries, battery contact inspection, blade removal, and known-good accessory testing, stop troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The device is removed from clinical use rather than being repeatedly tested in patient-care areas.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Ambu King Vision aBlade display would not power on during airway setup.
CCR.Cause: Fresh batteries were installed and the battery contacts, battery door, housing, and blade connection were checked, but the display remained blank.
CCR.Resolution: Device was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to suspected internal display or power-control failure.

helpfulDetails: Display blank, flickering, or briefly powering on
Any drop, cleaning, fluid exposure, or corrosion noted

title: Ambu King Vision aBlade Video Laryngoscope - Low Battery / Flashing Battery Indicator
description: Troubleshooting low battery warnings, flashing battery indicators, weak display power, or intermittent shutdown during King Vision aBlade use.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the King Vision aBlade is not being relied on for an active airway attempt before troubleshooting.

If the device is being used during a procedure and the battery indicator is flashing or the display becomes unstable, clinical staff should switch to another ready airway device immediately.

Expected outcome: Patient care is moved to a dependable device before battery-related troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Battery Indicator
steps.instructions:
Power on the display and observe the battery icon or flashing battery indicator.

Confirm whether the issue is:

Flashing battery icon
Low battery message or symbol
Display powers on briefly then shuts off
Display dims or flickers
Unit fails to power on after a low battery warning

Expected outcome: The battery-related symptom is confirmed and documented.

steps.title: Replace the Batteries With New Approved Batteries
steps.instructions:
Remove the battery compartment cover and replace the batteries with new, correct-type batteries as specified for the device.

Do not reuse partially depleted batteries from another device.

Expected outcome: The display powers on normally and the low battery indicator clears.

If this resolves the issue, return the device to service after functional verification and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Battery Orientation
steps.instructions:
Verify each battery is installed in the correct polarity direction.

Incorrect battery orientation may allow no power, intermittent power, or a continued low battery indication.

Expected outcome: Batteries are seated correctly and the display powers on without a flashing battery warning.

steps.title: Inspect the Battery Compartment
steps.instructions:
Remove the batteries and inspect the battery compartment for:

Corrosion
Bent or recessed contacts
Fluid residue
Loose springs
Cracked plastic
Evidence of cleaning solution intrusion

Expected outcome: Battery contacts are clean, dry, intact, and making proper contact.

If corrosion, fluid intrusion, or damaged contacts are found, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Verify the Battery Cover Secures Properly
steps.instructions:
Reinstall the battery cover and confirm it closes fully without looseness or movement.

A loose cover can allow momentary battery contact loss during handling.

Expected outcome: Battery compartment remains secure during normal handling.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions:
Turn the display off, wait briefly, and power it back on after installing fresh batteries.

Observe whether the low battery indicator returns immediately.

Expected outcome: The unit starts normally and the battery indicator remains stable.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Power During Gentle Handling
steps.instructions:
With fresh batteries installed, gently move the display as it would be handled during setup.

Do not drop, strike, or stress the device.

Watch for flickering, shutdown, or battery indicator changes.

Expected outcome: Display remains powered and stable during normal handling.

If the display flickers or shuts off, suspect battery contact damage or an internal power issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Display and Blade Connection Area
steps.instructions:
Confirm the aBlade connection area is clean, dry, and free of visible contamination.

A connection issue is not usually the primary cause of a low battery indicator, but poor connection or moisture can create confusing power or display behavior.

Expected outcome: Device connection points appear clean and undamaged.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions:
Attach a known-good compatible aBlade and power on the device.

Confirm:

Display turns on normally
Image appears
Battery indicator is stable
No flickering occurs
Device remains powered during normal movement

Expected outcome: The device passes a practical operational check before being returned to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Ambu King Vision aBlade display showed a flashing low battery indicator during airway setup.
CCR.Cause: Batteries were depleted, and the low battery indicator cleared after new batteries were installed with correct polarity.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced batteries, inspected battery contacts and cover, verified stable power and video image with a compatible aBlade, and returned the device to service.

title: Ambu King Vision aBlade Video Laryngoscope - No image after blade attachment
description: Troubleshooting no video image after attaching a King Vision aBlade due to blade seating, contacts, battery, display, or accessory issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the device fails during airway preparation or active airway management, clinical staff should immediately use another ready video laryngoscope or alternate airway device. Do not troubleshoot the Ambu King Vision aBlade on an active patient. Expected outcome: Patient care is transferred to a reliable airway visualization device before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Power on the display and attach the aBlade while observing the screen. Confirm whether the issue is: Completely black screen; Display powers on but shows no camera image; Image appears briefly, then drops out; Error or abnormal display behavior after blade attachment. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed and documented.

steps.title: Verify the Display Powers On Normally
steps.instructions: Turn the King Vision display on without the blade attached. Check for normal startup behavior, screen illumination, and battery indication if available. Expected outcome: The display powers on normally before evaluating the blade connection. If the display does not power on, stop this guide and troubleshoot the display power or battery condition first.

steps.title: Check the Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Replace the batteries with known-good, properly installed batteries if the display is dim, intermittent, or fails during blade attachment. Confirm the battery contacts are clean and that the battery compartment is fully closed. Expected outcome: The display remains powered on and stable during blade attachment. If replacing the batteries restores video, return the unit to service after functional verification.

steps.title: Remove and Reattach the aBlade
steps.instructions: Remove the aBlade and inspect how it seats onto the display. Reattach it firmly and evenly, making sure it is fully engaged and not angled, partially inserted, or loose. Expected outcome: The display detects the blade and produces a live camera image. If reseating the blade restores the image, verify stable operation with gentle handling and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Blade-to-Display Connection Area
steps.instructions: Look at the blade interface and display connection area for: Debris; Fluid residue; Bent or damaged contacts; Cracked plastic; Worn latch area; Signs of cleaning-fluid intrusion. Do not scrape contacts aggressively or use excessive liquid. Expected outcome: Connection surfaces are clean, dry, and physically intact.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good aBlade
steps.instructions: Attach a new or known-good compatible Ambu King Vision aBlade. Expected outcome: The display shows a clear image with the known-good blade. If the known-good blade works, remove the original blade from use and document the disposable blade failure.

steps.title: Check for Blade Damage or Packaging Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect the suspect aBlade for cracked housing, damaged camera window, loose connection area, or contamination from packaging or storage. Expected outcome: The blade is physically intact and suitable for use. If the blade appears damaged, discard it according to facility policy and verify the display with another blade.

steps.title: Verify the Display with Multiple Blades
steps.instructions: If one blade does not work, test the display with at least one additional known-good blade when available. Expected outcome: A properly functioning display should produce an image with known-good blades. If multiple known-good blades fail to produce an image, suspect the display or blade connector interface.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Image During Handling
steps.instructions: With a known-good blade attached, gently move the display and blade connection without stressing the device. Watch for flickering, image dropout, or loss of video. Expected outcome: Image remains stable during normal handling. If the image cuts in and out, remove the display from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect for External Damage
steps.instructions: Check the King Vision display for signs of impact, cracked housing, loose battery door, damaged screen, or contamination around the blade connection. Expected outcome: No external damage is present that would explain the connection failure. If damage is found, remove the display from service.

steps.title: Perform a Final Functional Check
steps.instructions: Attach a known-good blade and confirm: Display powers on; Blade is recognized; Live image appears; Image remains stable; Camera view is clear; No flicker or dropout occurs during normal handling. Expected outcome: The device is ready for safe clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Ambu King Vision aBlade produced no image after blade attachment.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found that the display powered on, but no video image appeared with the reported blade; known-good blade testing confirmed the original blade connection failed.
CCR.Resolution: Defective aBlade was removed from use, display passed functional check with a known-good blade, and unit was returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the display powered on normally
Battery condition or battery replacement performed
Whether the issue occurred with one blade or multiple blades
Blade lot number if a disposable blade issue is suspected
Any flickering or intermittent image behavior
Signs of fluid residue, debris, impact, or cracked housing
Whether a known-good blade restored the image

title: Ambu King Vision aBlade Video Laryngoscope - Blurred or Reversed Video Image
description: Troubleshooting blurred, distorted, upside-down, or reversed video caused by blade seating, lens contamination, display position, or device damage.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the King Vision aBlade is being used during an active airway attempt, do not continue troubleshooting while patient care is delayed. Have clinical staff move to an alternate airway device or backup video laryngoscope if the image is not immediately usable. Expected outcome: The patient is not dependent on a laryngoscope with an unreliable image.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Image Problem
steps.instructions: Power on the display and confirm whether the issue is: Blurry image; Fogged or obscured image; Dark or distorted image; Image appears reversed; Image orientation changes when the handle or display is moved. Expected outcome: The exact video issue is identified before parts are swapped.

steps.title: Check the aBlade for Lens Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the distal camera window on the disposable aBlade. Look for: Protective film left in place; Packaging residue; Fluid, blood, secretions, or cleaning residue; Scratches, cracks, or clouding on the camera window. If contamination is present, replace the disposable aBlade. Do not attempt to reuse a compromised disposable blade. Expected outcome: A clean, undamaged blade produces a clear image. If resolved, stop.

steps.title: Verify the Blade Is Fully Seated
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the aBlade onto the display/handle assembly. Confirm it locks into place evenly and does not feel loose, tilted, or partially engaged. Expected outcome: A properly seated blade provides a stable, correctly oriented image. If resolved, stop.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Disposable aBlade
steps.instructions: Install a new, unopened, compatible King Vision aBlade. This helps separate a blade-related camera issue from a display or handle issue. Expected outcome: If the new blade produces a clear, correctly oriented image, the original blade was defective, contaminated, or damaged. Remove the bad blade from use and stop.

steps.title: Check Display Position and Viewing Angle
steps.instructions: Confirm the image is not being interpreted as reversed because the display is rotated, viewed from an unusual angle, or held in an incorrect orientation. Reposition the display and compare the image to expected camera direction. Expected outcome: The displayed image should match expected blade movement and orientation.

steps.title: Inspect the Display/Handle Connection Area
steps.instructions: Check the connector area where the blade interfaces with the reusable display. Look for: Debris; Bent or damaged contacts; Moisture; Cracked plastic; Loose fit between blade and display. Do not scrape contacts aggressively or disassemble the unit. Expected outcome: The connector area is clean, dry, intact, and allows the blade to seat correctly.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions: Turn the display off, wait briefly, and power it back on with a known-good blade installed. Expected outcome: The image initializes normally. If the image returns to normal, document the event and verify repeat operation before returning to service.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition
steps.instructions: If the image is dim, unstable, delayed, or intermittently distorted, verify the battery condition or replace the battery if applicable to the device configuration. Low power can contribute to unreliable display behavior. Expected outcome: The display remains stable with adequate power.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Examine the display, hinge area, housing, screen, and blade interface for evidence of drops, impact, cracking, liquid intrusion, or loose components. Expected outcome: No visible damage is present. If damage is found, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check With Multiple Known-Good Blades
steps.instructions: Test the display with at least one additional known-good compatible blade if available. If blurred or reversed video occurs with multiple blades, the issue is likely with the reusable display/handle assembly. Expected outcome: The device consistently produces a clear, correctly oriented image before return to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Ambu King Vision aBlade image was blurry and appeared reversed during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: Found disposable aBlade camera window contaminated and blade not fully seated on the display assembly.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the aBlade, reseated the connection, verified clear and correctly oriented video image, and returned the display to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether a new disposable blade was tested
Any signs of fluid intrusion or impact damage
Whether the issue followed the blade or stayed with the display

title: Ambu King Vision aBlade Video Laryngoscope - Blade-to-display connection failure
description: Troubleshooting poor blade connection, no image, split image, frozen image, or display shutdown caused by blade, adapter, or contact issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the King Vision aBlade is being prepared for an airway case and the image is not reliable, do not continue with that device. Provide clinical staff with another ready video laryngoscope or alternate airway device before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed by an unreliable display or blade connection.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask staff what was observed: No image; Static or snow; Split image; Frozen image; Image drops out when the blade is moved; Display powers off after blade movement; Blade not recognized. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed before parts are swapped. Ambu quick-reference guidance notes that blade connection timing can affect image behavior, including split or frozen image conditions.

steps.title: Check That the Blade Is Connected Before Power-On
steps.instructions: Power the display off. Remove the blade and adapter, then reconnect them fully before powering the display back on. Expected outcome: The display powers up with a live moving image. If the image returns, document improper connection sequence or incomplete blade seating and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Disposable aBlade
steps.instructions: Check the blade for: Cracked plastic; Bent or damaged connector area; Contamination on the connector; Blood, cleaning residue, or moisture near the connection point; Packaging damage; Wrong blade size or wrong blade type for the adapter. Expected outcome: The blade is clean, intact, and compatible. If damage or contamination is found, discard the disposable blade and retest with a new blade. Ambu describes the King Vision aBlade system as using single-use blades.

steps.title: Inspect the aBlade Adapter
steps.instructions: Remove the aBlade adapter and inspect it externally. Look for: Cracked housing; Loose fit; Bent connector contacts; Debris in the connection channel; Signs of liquid intrusion; Excessive wear from repeated blade insertion. Expected outcome: The adapter seats securely and has no visible damage. If the adapter is damaged, remove it from use and replace or send for evaluation.

steps.title: Reseat the Display, Adapter, and Blade
steps.instructions: Reconnect the components in this order: Display off; Adapter fully seated to display; aBlade fully seated to adapter; Power display on. Gently move the handle and blade while watching the screen. Expected outcome: The image remains stable during light movement. If the image cuts out during movement, suspect a loose adapter, worn connection, or damaged display connection point.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Blade
steps.instructions: Use a new or known-good compatible aBlade. Power the display off before connecting the replacement blade. Expected outcome: A stable live image appears. If the issue resolves, the original blade was defective, contaminated, or improperly seated. Discard the failed disposable blade and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Adapter
steps.instructions: If available, test the display with a known-good compatible aBlade adapter. Expected outcome: The display recognizes the blade and maintains a stable image. If the issue resolves, replace the original adapter and document adapter failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Display Connection Area
steps.instructions: With the device removed from patient use, inspect the display stem connection area. Look for: Bent pins or contacts; Cracked plastic; Worn connector retention; Moisture or residue; Evidence of cleaning fluid exposure; Loose fit between display and adapter. Expected outcome: The display connection area is clean, dry, and mechanically secure. Do not probe aggressively or attempt internal repair at the bedside.

steps.title: Check for Cleaning or Liquid Exposure
steps.instructions: Ask whether the display was recently cleaned, dropped into fluid, or stored while wet. The King Vision display is not intended to be submerged, and connector openings should be protected from liquid exposure during cleaning. Expected outcome: No liquid contamination is present at the electrical connection. If liquid exposure is suspected, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Verify Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the display has adequate battery power. Low battery can complicate troubleshooting by causing shutdowns or unstable display behavior. Expected outcome: Battery condition is sufficient for testing. If battery status is low or questionable, replace batteries or use a known-good display according to facility practice, then retest.

steps.title: Perform a Final Functional Check
steps.instructions: With a known-good blade and adapter installed before power-on, confirm: Display powers on; Live image appears; Image does not freeze; Image does not split; Image remains stable with light handling; No visible damage is present; Device is clean and ready for clinical use. Expected outcome: The King Vision aBlade system passes a basic functional check. If the issue is resolved, return the device to service per facility policy.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Ambu King Vision aBlade display showed a frozen image after the blade was connected.
CCR.Cause: Found the disposable aBlade did not maintain a secure connection to the aBlade adapter, while a known-good blade worked normally.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the failed disposable blade from use, verified stable live image with a replacement blade, and returned the display and adapter to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the blade was connected before power-on
Whether the image was blank, split, frozen, or intermittent
Whether a known-good blade was tested
Whether a known-good adapter was tested
Any visible cracks or loose fit

title: Ambu aView 2 Advance Endoscopy Monitor - No Image / Black Screen After Scope Connection
description: Troubleshooting black screen or no image after connecting a compatible Ambu endoscope to the aView 2 Advance monitor.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the aView 2 Advance while it is being relied on for an active procedure. If the monitor or scope is needed for patient care, provide a known-good monitor and compatible scope before continuing. Expected outcome: Patient care is supported by a functioning visualization system. Why it matters: A black screen during endoscopy can delay visualization, airway assessment, or procedural decision-making.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Power on the aView 2 Advance and connect the reported scope. Confirm whether the monitor has: A completely black screen; A powered display with no camera image; A frozen image; A “no scope detected” or similar connection message; Normal menus but no live video. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified. If the image appears normally, document unable to duplicate and return the device only after functional verification.

steps.title: Verify Monitor Power and Startup
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor powers on fully and reaches the normal operating screen. Check for low battery, charging status, startup delays, or abnormal indicator behavior. Connect the monitor to approved power if battery level is low. Expected outcome: The monitor powers on normally and is not failing due to low power. If charging or power behavior is abnormal, stop troubleshooting the image complaint and evaluate the monitor for a power issue.

steps.title: Check Display Brightness and Screen Visibility
steps.instructions: Verify that the screen brightness is not set too low. Inspect the display under normal room lighting and reduce glare if needed. Expected outcome: Menus and on-screen indicators are visible. If the display itself remains black with no visible menu or status information, remove the monitor from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm Scope Compatibility
steps.instructions: Verify that the connected Ambu scope is compatible with the aView 2 Advance system. Do not assume all Ambu scopes use the same connection or software support. Expected outcome: The connected scope is appropriate for use with the monitor. If the scope is not compatible, use the correct scope/monitor combination and document the finding. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Scope Connector
steps.instructions: Disconnect the scope and inspect the connector for: Bent or damaged contacts; Fluid contamination; Debris; Cracked housing; Loose strain relief; Evidence of rough handling. Expected outcome: The scope connector is clean, dry, and physically intact. If connector damage or contamination is found, remove the scope from use and test the monitor with a known-good compatible scope.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor Scope Port
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the aView 2 Advance scope input port. Look for: Obstruction; Bent pins or damaged contacts; Foreign material; Fluid intrusion; Cracked or loose port housing. Expected outcome: The monitor input port appears clean and undamaged. If the port is damaged or contaminated, remove the monitor from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Reconnect the Scope Correctly
steps.instructions: Reconnect the scope fully and securely. Confirm that the connector seats completely without forcing it. Avoid twisting, angling, or partially inserting the connector. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the scope and displays a live image. If the image returns, the issue was likely incomplete seating. Document the correction and verify operation before return to service.

steps.title: Power Cycle With Scope Connected
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off, leave the compatible scope connected, then power the monitor back on. Observe whether the monitor detects the scope during startup. Expected outcome: The monitor completes startup and displays the scope image. If the image returns after reboot, perform several disconnect/reconnect checks before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Compatible Scope
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible Ambu scope to the same aView 2 Advance monitor. Expected outcome: The monitor displays a live image from the known-good scope. If the known-good scope works, the original scope is the likely cause. Remove the original scope from use and document the failed accessory.

steps.title: Test the Reported Scope on a Known-Good Monitor
steps.instructions: When available, connect the reported scope to another compatible known-good Ambu monitor. Expected outcome: The scope displays an image on the alternate monitor. If the scope works on another monitor, the original aView 2 Advance monitor is likely the issue and should be removed from service.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Connection Behavior
steps.instructions: With a test scope connected and not in patient use, gently move the connector and cable near the scope plug. Do not sharply bend or stress the cable. Watch for: Image loss; Flicker; Scope disconnect messages; Freezing; Black screen returning. Expected outcome: The image remains stable during light handling. If movement causes image loss, suspect connector, cable, or input port damage. Remove the affected item from service.

steps.title: Inspect for Scope Lens or Distal Tip Obstruction
steps.instructions: If the monitor is powered and the image area appears dark rather than truly disconnected, inspect the scope’s distal tip. Check for a covered lens, packaging material, debris, fluid, or damage. Expected outcome: The camera lens is unobstructed and produces a visible image. If clearing the obstruction restores the image, document the cause and verify image quality before return to use.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or System Prompts
steps.instructions: Review the monitor screen for any connection, software, compatibility, or accessory-related message. Note the exact wording in the work order. Expected outcome: Any system message helps identify whether the issue is scope detection, video signal, compatibility, or monitor hardware. If the monitor displays a recurring error with multiple known-good scopes, remove it from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Final Operation
steps.instructions: Before returning the device, verify: Monitor powers on normally; Compatible scope is detected; Live image displays; Image remains stable during normal handling; No abnormal error messages appear; Battery or power status is acceptable. Expected outcome: The aView 2 Advance and scope pass basic functional verification. If all checks pass, return the device to service and document the results.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Ambu aView 2 Advance displayed a black screen after connecting an Ambu scope.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the reported scope failed to produce an image on the aView 2 Advance, while a known-good compatible scope displayed normally.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the failed scope from service, verified the aView 2 Advance with a known-good compatible scope, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Battery or AC power status checked
Reported scope tested on another monitor, if available
Image behavior during light cable movement
Environmental factors such as lighting, glare, fluid exposure, or room conditions

title: Ambu aView 2 Advance Endoscopy Monitor - Scope Not Detected or Connection Error
description: Troubleshooting scope detection, connection, and image recognition issues on the Ambu aView 2 Advance endoscopy monitor.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the scope is connected to a patient or is being prepared for an active procedure, do not continue troubleshooting on that patient setup. Move clinical staff to another approved monitor, scope, or backup visualization system before testing. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on a monitor or scope connection that may be unreliable. Why it matters: A scope detection failure can delay visualization during airway, endoscopy, or bedside procedures.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Complaint
steps.instructions: Power on the aView 2 Advance and confirm the exact issue. Check whether the monitor shows: No scope detected, Connection error, No image after scope connection, Intermittent detection, Error only with one specific scope, Error on all compatible scopes. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly confirmed before parts or accessories are blamed.

steps.title: Confirm the Scope Type Is Compatible
steps.instructions: Verify that the connected Ambu single-use scope is intended for use with the aView 2 Advance. Do not assume all Ambu scopes or older aScope models are compatible with every Ambu display platform. Expected outcome: A compatible scope is being used. If an incompatible scope is found, replace it with the correct compatible scope and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Scope Packaging and Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the scope is new, undamaged, and within its expiration date. Inspect the scope connector, cable, strain relief, and distal tip for visible damage before connection. Expected outcome: The scope appears physically intact and suitable for testing. If the scope is damaged, expired, contaminated, or previously used, do not troubleshoot further with that scope. Replace it and stop.

steps.title: Check the Monitor Input Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the aView 2 Advance scope connection port for: Debris, Bent or damaged contacts, Fluid contamination, Cracked plastic, Loose port fit, Signs of forced insertion. Expected outcome: The port is clean, dry, and physically intact. If fluid, damage, or loose port movement is found, remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Reconnect the Scope Correctly
steps.instructions: Disconnect the scope and reconnect it firmly using the correct orientation. Do not force the connector. Watch the display during connection to see whether the scope is recognized. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the scope and displays an image. If reconnecting resolves the issue, document the loose or incomplete connection and return the device only after confirming stable operation.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Disconnect the scope. Power the aView 2 Advance off, wait briefly, then power it back on. After the monitor fully starts, reconnect the scope. Expected outcome: The monitor initializes normally and detects the connected scope. If detection returns after restart, verify several reconnects before returning the monitor to service.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Compatible Scope
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible Ambu scope, preferably from unopened stock. Confirm whether the aView 2 Advance detects the known-good scope. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the known-good scope. If the known-good scope works, the original scope is likely defective or damaged. Remove the original scope from use and follow facility process for defective disposable accessories.

steps.title: Test the Suspect Scope on Another Known-Good Monitor
steps.instructions: If available and appropriate, connect the suspect scope to another compatible aView 2 Advance or approved Ambu display. Expected outcome: The second monitor either detects the scope or repeats the failure. If the scope fails on another monitor, the scope is the likely cause. Stop using that scope. If the scope works on another monitor, continue evaluating the original monitor.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Connection
steps.instructions: With a test scope connected and not in patient use, gently observe the cable and connector area without stressing the disposable scope. Watch for image dropouts, detection loss, or connection messages when the connector is lightly handled. Expected outcome: The connection remains stable. If detection drops in and out, suspect monitor input-port wear, connector damage, or accessory connection instability. Remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Confirm Monitor Power and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: If the monitor is running on battery, connect it to approved power or docking/charging equipment if applicable. Confirm the monitor is not shutting down, rebooting, or displaying low-power behavior during scope connection. Expected outcome: The monitor remains powered and stable during startup and scope detection. If the monitor reboots, freezes, or loses power during connection, remove it from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Software or Startup Abnormalities
steps.instructions: Observe whether the monitor starts normally before the scope is connected. Look for frozen screen behavior, delayed boot, repeated error messages, or touchscreen/menu issues. Expected outcome: The monitor user interface is responsive before scope testing. If the monitor is unstable before any scope is connected, the issue is likely monitor-related and should be escalated.

steps.title: Clean Only External Surfaces as Approved
steps.instructions: If the connector area appears dirty externally, clean only according to facility-approved and manufacturer-approved external cleaning practices. Do not spray liquid directly into the port. Allow the device to dry fully before retesting. Expected outcome: No visible external contamination remains, and the port is dry before testing. If fluid intrusion is suspected, remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Perform a Final Functional Check
steps.instructions: After any corrective action, connect a compatible known-good scope and confirm: Scope is detected, Image appears, Image remains stable, No connection error returns, Monitor remains powered, Touchscreen or controls respond normally, No physical port looseness is present. Expected outcome: The aView 2 Advance reliably detects the scope and maintains image display. If the issue is resolved, document the findings and return the monitor to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Ambu aView 2 Advance displayed a scope connection error and would not detect the connected disposable scope.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the original scope failed detection on the aView 2 Advance, while a known-good compatible scope was detected normally.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the defective disposable scope, verified stable image display with a known-good compatible scope, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Scope lot number if disposable accessory failure is suspected
Whether the scope was new, expired, damaged, or previously opened
Whether a known-good scope was tested
Whether the suspect scope worked on another monitor
Battery or power status during testing
Any rebooting, freezing, or touchscreen issues
Whether the connection was intermittent or completely failed

title: Ambu aView 2 Advance Endoscopy Monitor - Poor, Blurry, or Intermittent Video Image
description: Troubleshooting poor, blurry, frozen, flickering, or intermittent video image issues caused by scope, cable, display, settings, or connection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Ambu aView 2 Advance is being used during an active procedure and the image becomes poor, blurry, frozen, or intermittent, notify clinical staff immediately. Do not continue troubleshooting on an active patient unless clinical staff confirms it is safe. Provide an alternate visualization device or backup monitor if the image cannot be trusted. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: Endoscopy and airway visualization depend on a clear, stable image. An unreliable image can delay or compromise clinical decisions.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Image Problem
steps.instructions: Power on the aView 2 Advance and connect the reported Ambu endoscope or visualization device. Confirm the exact symptom: Blurry image; Poor contrast or washed-out image; Flickering image; Intermittent image loss; Frozen image; Delayed video; No image after movement of the cable or scope. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed or cannot be reproduced. If the image is normal and stable during testing, document that the issue could not be duplicated and return the device only after appropriate functional checks.

steps.title: Check Whether the Problem Follows the Endoscope
steps.instructions: Disconnect the reported endoscope and connect a known-good compatible Ambu endoscope or test device, if available. Observe whether the image becomes clear and stable. Expected outcome: A known-good scope produces a normal image. If the known-good scope works normally, the original endoscope is likely the issue. Remove that endoscope from use if reusable, or discard it if single-use per facility policy. Stop troubleshooting the monitor. Why it matters: Image problems are often caused by the camera, lens, connector, or cable of the attached endoscope rather than the monitor itself.

steps.title: Inspect the Endoscope Lens or Distal Tip
steps.instructions: Check the distal lens area for contamination, fogging, fluid, scratches, cracks, dried debris, or packaging residue. Clean or replace the scope according to facility policy and the device type. Expected outcome: The image improves after the lens or distal tip issue is corrected. If the image becomes clear, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check the Scope-to-Monitor Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the aView 2 Advance input port and the scope connector for: Incomplete seating; Bent pins; Damaged contacts; Fluid contamination; Debris; Loose fit; Strain at the connector. Reconnect the scope firmly without forcing the connector. Expected outcome: The image appears and remains stable after reseating the connection. If reseating resolves the issue, perform a gentle movement check and document the connector condition.

steps.title: Perform a Cable Movement Check
steps.instructions: With the device not connected to a patient, gently move the scope cable near the connector, along the cable length, and near the distal end. Watch for image flicker, freezing, color changes, or loss of video. Expected outcome: The image remains stable during gentle movement. If the image cuts in and out when the cable is moved, remove the affected scope or cable assembly from service. Do not continue using it clinically. Why it matters: Intermittent video is commonly caused by damaged conductors, connector strain, or cable fatigue.

steps.title: Check Monitor Display Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the aView 2 Advance screen for cracks, pressure damage, liquid intrusion, dim areas, discoloration, or touch/display abnormalities. Clean the screen using approved cleaning methods and verify that the image issue is not simply external screen contamination. Expected outcome: The display is physically intact and the image quality is not affected by surface contamination. If the screen is damaged, remove the monitor from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Brightness and Display Settings
steps.instructions: Check that the monitor brightness, display mode, and image settings have not been adjusted in a way that makes the image appear dim, washed out, or difficult to interpret. Restore settings to normal clinical use if they were changed. Expected outcome: Image brightness and contrast are appropriate for clinical viewing. If correcting settings resolves the complaint, document the setting change and stop.

steps.title: Check Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the aView 2 Advance has adequate battery charge or is connected to approved external power. Observe whether the image issue occurs when the battery is low, when charging, or when the power adapter is moved. Expected outcome: The monitor remains powered and the video remains stable. If video instability is associated with low battery or power interruptions, charge the monitor fully, inspect the power adapter, and retest.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor
steps.instructions: Power the aView 2 Advance off, wait briefly, and power it back on. Reconnect the scope and verify whether the video feed returns to normal. Expected outcome: The monitor boots normally and displays a stable image. If the issue clears after restart but returns repeatedly, document the behavior and escalate for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Test Multiple Known-Good Scopes
steps.instructions: If available, test more than one known-good compatible scope with the monitor. Expected outcome: All known-good scopes provide a clear and stable image. If multiple known-good scopes show the same poor or intermittent image on the same monitor, the issue is likely with the aView 2 Advance monitor, input port, display electronics, or internal processing. Remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Confirm Final Device Status
steps.instructions: After troubleshooting, clearly determine whether the issue was caused by: Original scope; Scope lens contamination; Loose connection; Damaged cable; Display settings; Low battery or power problem; Monitor hardware issue. Expected outcome: The device status is documented clearly before return to service or escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Ambu aView 2 Advance had a blurry and intermittent video image during setup for endoscopy use.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found that the image dropped out when the connected scope cable was gently moved near the monitor connector.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the affected scope from service, verified the aView 2 Advance produced a stable image with a known-good compatible scope, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue occurred during setup or during a procedure
Whether a known-good scope was tested
Whether the image was blurry, flickering, frozen, delayed, or intermittent
Any visible contamination, fluid, debris, or damage
Whether the issue followed the scope or stayed with the monitor

title: Ambu aView 2 Advance Endoscopy Monitor - Battery Will Not Charge or Monitor Shuts Down Quickly
description: Troubleshooting charging failure, short battery runtime, unexpected shutdown, power adapter issues, and external charging-related problems on the Ambu aView 2 Advance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the aView 2 Advance is being used for an active procedure, do not troubleshoot the battery or charging issue during patient care. Move the clinical team to a known-good monitor or alternate visualization system before continuing. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on a monitor that may shut down unexpectedly. Why it matters: A sudden monitor shutdown during endoscopy or airway visualization can interrupt clinical workflow and create a patient safety risk.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Battery Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the monitor and verify the exact complaint. Check whether the issue is: Battery will not charge; Battery percentage does not increase; Monitor shuts down quickly on battery; Monitor only works while connected to AC power; Charging indicator does not appear; Unit powers off during startup or shortly after unplugging. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed before replacing accessories or escalating.

steps.title: Connect to a Known-Good AC Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug the charger into a verified working wall outlet. Avoid power strips, loose receptacles, switched outlets, or outlets controlled by wall switches. Expected outcome: The monitor shows charging activity or powers reliably from AC. If this resolves the issue, stop. The likely cause was outlet or facility power-related.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Adapter and Charging Cable
steps.instructions: Check the AC adapter, power cord, and connector for: Bent or damaged pins; Cracked housing; Frayed cable; Loose strain relief; Signs of overheating; Intermittent power when the cable is moved. Expected outcome: The charger and cable appear intact and provide stable power. Why it matters: A damaged charger may power the monitor intermittently but fail to charge the battery correctly.

steps.title: Use a Known-Good Compatible Charger
steps.instructions: If available, connect the aView 2 Advance to a known-good compatible Ambu-approved power supply. Allow the monitor to remain connected long enough to observe whether the battery icon or charge status changes. Expected outcome: The monitor begins charging normally. If this resolves the issue, stop. Remove the failed charger from service and replace it.

steps.title: Check the Monitor Charging Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the charging port without inserting tools or forcing the connector. Look for: Debris; Fluid contamination; Loose connection; Physical damage; Connector movement; Charging dropouts when the cable is gently repositioned. Expected outcome: The charging port is clean, dry, stable, and accepts the connector securely. If the port is damaged or loose, remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Verify the Monitor Is Fully Seated on External Power
steps.instructions: Confirm the power connector is fully inserted and not partially engaged. Observe whether the charging indicator changes when the connector is gently seated. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes external power consistently. Why it matters: A partially seated connector can mimic a battery or internal charging failure.

steps.title: Allow a Depleted Battery Recovery Period
steps.instructions: If the monitor battery is fully depleted, leave it connected to a known-good power source for a reasonable charging period before retesting. Then attempt to power the unit on while still connected to AC. Expected outcome: The monitor powers on and shows charging progress. If the monitor remains dead or does not recognize charging, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Rapid Shutdown After Charging
steps.instructions: After the monitor has charged, disconnect AC power and observe runtime behavior. Confirm whether the battery percentage drops abnormally fast or the monitor shuts down shortly after unplugging. Expected outcome: Battery runtime is stable enough for normal clinical use. If the monitor shuts down quickly after charging, suspect battery degradation or internal power management failure.

steps.title: Check for Overheating, Odor, or Battery Swelling Signs
steps.instructions: Inspect the monitor exterior for abnormal heat, odor, case distortion, swelling, or unusual display behavior during charging. Expected outcome: The unit remains physically normal during charging. If heat, odor, swelling, or case distortion is present, disconnect power immediately and remove the device from service.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: With the monitor disconnected from accessories, power the unit off completely, reconnect AC power, and power it back on. Observe whether charging status returns to normal. Expected outcome: A temporary software or power-state issue clears. If this resolves the issue, monitor the unit long enough to confirm stable charging before returning it to service.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Peripheral Influence
steps.instructions: Disconnect any attached endoscope, USB device, external media, or nonessential accessory. Retest charging and battery runtime with only the monitor and charger connected. Expected outcome: Charging behavior remains stable without accessories attached. Why it matters: A connected accessory or port issue may increase power draw or interfere with normal operation.

steps.title: Review Battery Age and Usage History
steps.instructions: Check service history, prior work orders, and user reports for repeated short-runtime complaints. Look for patterns such as: Repeated charging failures; Frequent unexpected shutdowns; Battery not holding charge after full charge; Unit stored unplugged for long periods; Prior charger replacements. Expected outcome: History supports whether this is a one-time external issue or a recurring battery/internal power fault.

steps.title: Perform Facility-Approved Functional Verification
steps.instructions: If the monitor charges normally after external checks, perform a basic operational check per facility process. Confirm: Monitor powers on; Display remains stable; Charging indicator appears; Battery percentage increases; Unit remains powered on battery for an acceptable observation period; No alarms, abnormal heat, or intermittent power behavior occur. Expected outcome: The monitor is safe to return to service if all checks pass.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Ambu aView 2 Advance would not hold a charge and powered off shortly after being unplugged.
CCR.Cause: Verified outlet and charger were functional, but the monitor battery percentage dropped rapidly after charging and the unit shut down during battery operation.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the monitor from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation due to suspected internal battery or charging-system failure.

helpfulDetails: Battery percentage before and after charging
Whether monitor works on AC power
Any unusual heat, odor, swelling, or case damage
Whether the issue occurs with accessories disconnected

title: Ambu aView 2 Advance Endoscopy Monitor - USB / Export, Recording, or Image Capture Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed USB export, image capture, video recording, or saved-file retrieval issues on the Ambu aView 2 Advance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is not being actively used for a patient procedure before troubleshooting recording, export, or capture issues. If the monitor is needed for active visualization, provide another functional monitor before removing it from service. Expected outcome: Patient care is not interrupted by troubleshooting activity. Why it matters: Image capture and export are secondary functions, but the monitor must remain reliable for live visualization during procedures.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what failed: Still image capture; Video recording; USB export; File playback; Saved image retrieval; USB device recognition; Export completes but files are missing or unreadable. Attempt to reproduce the issue using a test scope or approved test setup, not during patient use. Expected outcome: The specific failure mode is confirmed. If the issue cannot be reproduced and saved files/export operate normally, document findings and return the monitor to service.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Is Fully Powered and Stable
steps.instructions: Check that the aView 2 Advance is operating on stable power. If using AC power, verify the power adapter is fully seated and connected to a known-good outlet. If operating on battery, confirm the battery has adequate charge before attempting recording or export. Expected outcome: The monitor remains powered without interruption during capture, recording, or export. Why it matters: File writing or USB export may fail if the monitor loses power or shuts down during the process.

steps.title: Check Available Storage or Recording Capacity
steps.instructions: Review whether the monitor has available internal storage or whether prior recordings/images are filling memory. Delete old non-required test files only if allowed by facility policy and clinical documentation requirements. Expected outcome: The monitor has sufficient storage available for new images or recordings. If storage was full and clearing approved files restores capture or recording, document the resolution and stop.

steps.title: Verify Correct Capture or Recording Workflow
steps.instructions: Confirm the user is pressing the correct image capture or recording control and allowing the action to complete. Watch for on-screen confirmation, recording indicators, file-save messages, or error messages. Expected outcome: The monitor confirms that the image or video was saved. If no save confirmation appears, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check the Connected Endoscope or Camera Source
steps.instructions: Confirm that a compatible Ambu endoscope is connected and that a live image is displayed before attempting capture or recording. Reseat the scope connector if safe to do so during testing. Inspect the connector area for contamination, bent contacts, loose fit, or obvious damage. Expected outcome: A stable live image is present before capture or recording is attempted. Why it matters: Some capture or recording functions may fail or be unavailable if the monitor does not recognize a valid video source.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off using the normal shutdown method. Disconnect accessories and USB devices. Wait briefly, then power the monitor back on and reconnect only the required test scope. Attempt image capture and recording again. Expected outcome: Temporary software or file-handling issues clear after restart. If capture or recording works after restart, document the reset and verify repeated operation before returning to service.

steps.title: Inspect the USB Port Externally
steps.instructions: Check the USB port for: Bent or recessed contacts; Broken plastic insert; Debris or contamination; Loose fit; Signs of liquid exposure; Evidence the USB device was forced in incorrectly. Do not insert tools into the port unless following approved cleaning procedures. Expected outcome: The USB port is clean, intact, and physically secure. If the port is damaged, remove the monitor from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good USB Drive
steps.instructions: Use a known-good USB flash drive that is approved by the facility. Avoid encrypted, password-protected, damaged, or unusually large-capacity drives if they are not known to work with the device. Insert the drive fully and wait for the monitor to recognize it. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the USB drive and allows export. If export works with a different USB drive, the original USB device is the likely cause. Document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check USB Drive Format and Available Space
steps.instructions: Verify the USB drive has available storage space and is formatted in a compatible format per facility practice and manufacturer guidance. Remove unrelated files if needed or use a clean approved USB drive. Expected outcome: The USB device has enough capacity and can accept exported files. If a full or incompatible USB drive was the issue, replace it with an approved drive and document the resolution.

steps.title: Repeat Export With a Small Test File
steps.instructions: Capture a new test image or short test recording. Attempt to export only that file. Expected outcome: A small test file exports successfully. Why it matters: Large recordings may fail due to storage limits, file corruption, USB drive capacity, or export timeout conditions.

steps.title: Check for File Visibility on a Computer
steps.instructions: After export, safely remove the USB drive if the monitor provides that option. Insert the USB drive into a hospital-approved computer and confirm whether files are present and readable. Expected outcome: Exported files appear on the USB drive and open normally. If files are present but cannot be opened, document the file type, file size, and computer/application used.

steps.title: Check Date, Time, and File Naming Behavior
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor date and time are correct. Check whether files are saved under unexpected timestamps, folders, or naming formats. Expected outcome: Captured images and recordings can be located using the correct date/time and file structure. If incorrect date/time caused staff to think files were missing, correct the settings if permitted and document the finding.

steps.title: Test With Another Known-Good Monitor or Accessory Setup
steps.instructions: If available, compare operation using another Ambu aView 2 Advance monitor, the same USB drive, and the same test scope. Expected outcome: The issue follows either the monitor, USB device, or accessory setup. If the same USB drive works on another monitor but not this unit, suspect a monitor USB/export fault.

steps.title: Review for Software Lockup or Repeated Error Messages
steps.instructions: Note any recurring messages, frozen screens, failed save confirmations, export progress failures, or reboot behavior. Do not attempt software reloads, internal service procedures, or board-level repair unless authorized by service documentation and facility policy. Expected outcome: Any repeatable software or export fault is documented clearly for repair escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Ambu aView 2 Advance would not export captured procedure images to a USB drive.
CCR.Cause: Verified that the original USB drive was not recognized by the monitor, while a known-good approved USB drive exported test images successfully.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the USB drive with an approved working device, confirmed image capture and export, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue affected image capture, video recording, USB export, or file playback
Exact error message or on-screen behavior
Whether live endoscope image was present
USB drive brand, size, and format if known
Whether a known-good USB drive was tested
Whether old files were deleted per policy
Whether files were visible on a computer after export
Any freezing, rebooting, or failed save/export messages
